title: Legalizing the revolution: India and the constitution of the postcolony [Book Review]
author: Marwah

opening_move.description: The review opens with the provocative thesis of Dasgupta's book: the postcolonial revolution failed, and its failure is legible in the constitutional project that converted mass democratic energy into top-down bureaucratic state-building.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding how the constitutional project contained rather than channelled popular sovereignty in India, and why the anticolonial promise was domesticated

theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, presenting Dasgupta's critical-genealogical argument about the containment of popular sovereignty in Indian constitutional history.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: intellectual and legal history; genealogical analysis of constitutional design
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review of a historical-political theory work. Dasgupta's book itself uses archival and historical evidence; the review presents this without independent empirical engagement.

case_type.case_name: Indian independence movement and Constituent Assembly (1947 onwards)

confidence_notes: Book review genre; primarily expository. High normative stake reflects the political-theoretical argument of the reviewed book, not independent reviewer intervention.
